local_names.romanized_local: Mù Guā (Chinese); Betik (Malay); Pappāḷi (Tamil); Papītā (Hindi); Malakor (Thai)

region_of_origin: Native to Central America and Mexico; widely cultivated in Southeast Asia, India, Brazil, and Thailand; the Solo/Hawaiian variety is the standard supermarket papaya in Singapore
food_subgroup: Ripe tropical fruit — sweet orange-red flesh, green to yellow-orange skin, black seeds; Solo/Hawaiian variety

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ripe papaya fruit as a food item in available reference; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

main_ingredients: ripe papaya flesh (Carica papaya, Solo/Hawaiian variety)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 1 cup of papaya chunks (about 150 g), providing roughly 65 kcal and a glycemic load of approximately 10.

nutrient_source.title: Papayas, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169926); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database for ripe papaya
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy / Health Promotion Board Singapore (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169926 (Papayas, raw) is a direct match for ripe papaya flesh. Values cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo for regional alignment. All per-100g values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Papaya — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~60 (medium) for ripe papaya as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (60 × 11.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 9.9. This is a medium GI food; patients with T2DM should observe portion control.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (8 mg/100 g), good potassium (182 mg/100 g) and magnesium (21 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Ripe papaya is beneficial for hypertension management: its low sodium content, combined with meaningful potassium (182 mg/100 g) and magnesium (21 mg/100 g), supports blood pressure regulation, and its Vitamin C (61.8 mg/100 g) contributes to endothelial health — a standard 150 g serving is appropriate without restriction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~60) — higher than many patients expect; glycemic load ~10 per 150 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Papaya has a medium GI (~60), which is higher than many T2DM patients assume; a controlled 1-cup (150 g) serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 10 (moderate and manageable), but eating half a Solo papaya (300–500 g) in one sitting can deliver GL 18–33 — a significant glycemic event that should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high Vitamin C and beta-carotene antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ripe papaya is cardioprotective: it contains negligible saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and provides Vitamin C (61.8 mg/100 g) and beta-carotene (Vitamin A 47 mcg RAE/100 g) which support vascular health and reduce oxidative stress — regular moderate consumption is consistent with a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Papain enzyme (proteolytic, anti-inflammatory); beta-carotene antioxidant; folate beneficial for methotrexate users
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ripe papaya retains some papain (a proteolytic enzyme with demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties in vitro and limited clinical evidence), and its folate content (38 mcg/100 g) is beneficial for patients on methotrexate therapy — moderate regular consumption is appropriate.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ripe papaya (木瓜 / betik / பப்பாளி / มะละกอ) is one of Singapore's most widely consumed tropical fruits, available year-round in supermarkets and hawker fruit stalls at affordable prices. The Solo/Hawaiian variety — the standard Singapore supermarket papaya — is a small, single-serving fruit with green-to-yellow-orange skin, soft sweet orange-red flesh, and a cluster of black seeds at the centre. Its flavour is mildly sweet with a characteristic musky tropical scent; it is typically eaten fresh, sometimes with a squeeze of lime juice. Nutritionally, ripe papaya is notable for its beta-carotene (pro-Vitamin A, 47 mcg RAE/100 g), Vitamin C (61.8 mg/100 g), folate (38 mcg/100 g), and potassium (182 mg/100 g). It is low in calories (43 kcal/100 g), very low in fat (0.3 g/100 g), and low in sodium (8 mg/100 g). The key clinical counselling point is glycemic index: papaya has a medium GI (~60), which is higher than most patients assume. Many Singapore T2DM patients regard papaya as a 'safe health fruit' and eat it freely, but a half-papaya serving (300–500 g) can deliver 18–33 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of 11–20 — a meaningful glycemic event. A controlled 1-cup (150 g) portion with GL ~10 is appropriate for T2DM patients. Green papaya (unripe, used in Thai Som Tum and Malay cooking) is nutritionally a vegetable — very low in sugar — and is nutritionally distinct from ripe papaya. Papain, a proteolytic enzyme concentrated in unripe papaya and the latex, is retained in smaller amounts in ripe flesh and has anti-inflammatory properties relevant to arthritis management.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169926 (Papayas, raw), an approved primary source, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from approved sources. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are confirmed real codes for papaya.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA and PUFA fractions) for ripe papaya is not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169926 or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-13; these values are therefore unavailable from approved sources. water_g set to -1: moisture content for ripe papaya is not reported as a discrete field in the USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169926 record consulted or in HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-13; value is therefore unavailable from approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — ripe papaya contains no trans fats by nature). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — papaya is a plant food containing no cholesterol). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — papaya is a plant food containing no vitamin B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy — ripe papaya contains no meaningful vitamin D). All core macros (protein_g 0.5, fat_g 0.3, carbohydrate_g 11.0) are non-zero and sourced from USDA SR Legacy. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (60 × 11.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 9.9 for a 150 g serving. SNOMED CT code not identified for ripe papaya as a food item; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient for ontological alignment.
